what_the_source_actually_says:
The page LOADS (HTTP 200, full text renders). Title verbatim: "Completing the First Falcon Signature Verification in Starkware - Initiating the Transition to a Quantum-Safe Ethereum". Visible date on page: "Sep 18, 2023" — NOT 2024.

BTQ's own "first" language, verbatim: they describe "the first successful STARK-based verification of post-quantum digital signatures on Starknet", and "This achievement marks the first step in supporting NIST standardized post-quantum digital signature algorithms on Ethereum and replacing vulnerable cryptographic primitives with quantum-safe algorithms." So "first" IS self-asserted by BTQ (vendor blog, not a peer-reviewed or third-party claim), and it is scoped two ways: (a) "on Starknet", (b) "first STEP", not "first ever".

Scheme/parameter set: the page says "Falcon" only. Falcon-512 is NOT mentioned anywhere on the page — the parameter set is not published. Cannot determine from this source whether it was Falcon-512 or Falcon-1024.

Cairo vs. actually-proven: the page shows an image captioned "Cairo profile of Falcon signature verification" and says "This STARK implementation is based on our prior work on quantum-safe signature aggregation using PQScale, one of our core technologies at BTQ." The wording describes a completed Cairo implementation and a Cairo profile; it does NOT explicitly state that a STARK proof was generated, submitted, or verified on Starknet mainnet/testnet. Treat "a proof was actually produced" as NOT established by this source.

Performance numbers: NO proving time, NO Cairo step count, NO constraint count, NO RAM, NO gas. The only numbers on the page are aggregation/compression claims tied to PQScale, not prover cost: "12.5x" space savings, an aggregate "ten times smaller than Falcon signatures", a claim that PQScale brings "the per-signature cost of Falcon to below the cost of ECDSA", and a claim they can "roll up the verification of several thousands of Falcon signatures in a proof". These are vendor-claimed, unbenchmarked, and none of them is a prover-cost measurement.

EARLIER WORK (independent check): IACR ePrint 2021/1048, "Aggregating and thresholdizing hash-based signatures using STARKs" (Khaburzaniya, Chalkias, Lewi, Malvai), received 2021-08-16, last revised 2022-03-14 — i.e. ~2 years BEFORE the BTQ post — already verifies post-quantum (hash-based: Lamport, HORST, BPQS) signatures inside a STARK, with published prover benchmarks. It contains no mention of Falcon or any lattice scheme. So BTQ's broad phrasing "first successful STARK-based verification of post-quantum digital signatures" is CONTRADICTED as a general claim; it survives only under the narrow reading "first on Starknet / first for a lattice (Falcon) scheme in Cairo".

I found no evidence of any STARK or SNARK verification of Falcon specifically that predates 2023-09-18. The nearest lattice-proof-system work on Falcon (Aardal et al., compact aggregation of Falcon via LaBRADOR) is CRYPTO 2024, i.e. LATER, and is a lattice SNARK not a STARK/Cairo.

Also found, and relevant to positioning: an independent, later, more-quantitative Falcon-512-on-Starknet implementation by @feltroidPrime at https://www.s2morrow.xyz/ (code: https://github.com/feltroidprime/s2morrow), which DOES publish figures — "~9.5M L2 gas" for signature verification, "17x calldata compression (1,030 felts reduced to 62 felts on-chain)", "29 felt252 slots for 512 Zq coefficients", "65% less gas than secp256r1 syscall", "2x the cost of garaga ECDSA". No page date was visible to me and no Cairo step count or prover wall-clock is published, so its date needs a separate check before citing.

The arXiv SOK "Quantum Disruption" (https://arxiv.org/html/2512.13333v1) cites the BTQ work verbatim as "BTQ, in collaboration with StarkWare, demonstrated the first verification of FALCON signature on StarkNet, an Ethereum Layer-2 network" — so the "first" framing HAS been laundered into third-party literature, but that SOK cites no primary evidence beyond the BTQ blog itself and cites no earlier STARK/SNARK PQ-signature work at all.
verbatim_numbers: "12.5x" (space savings, PQScale aggregation); "ten times smaller than Falcon signatures" (aggregate size); "several thousands of Falcon signatures in a proof" (rollup capacity claim); "2024" (year of expected NIST standardization). Proving time: not published. Cairo steps: not published. Constraint count: not published. Peak RAM: not published. Proof size: not published. Gas: not published. — From the separate s2morrow page (different author, not BTQ): "~9.5M L2 gas", "17x calldata compression (1,030 felts reduced to 62 felts on-chain)", "29 felt252 slots for 512 Zq coefficients", "65% less gas than secp256r1 syscall", "2x the cost of garaga ECDSA".
discrepancies:
FOUR discrepancies, one of them load-bearing:

1. DATE IS WRONG. The brief says 2024. The page says "Sep 18, 2023". If we publish "BTQ + StarkWare did Falcon on Starknet in 2024" we are off by a year and anyone who clicks the link sees it immediately. Fix to 2023.

2. THE CLAIM IS MISATTRIBUTED IN SHAPE. The brief renders it as "completed the FIRST STARK-based verification of a Falcon signature". BTQ's actual words are "the first successful STARK-based verification of post-quantum digital signatures on Starknet" — broader in scheme (post-quantum signatures generally, not "a Falcon signature") and narrower in venue (explicitly "on Starknet"). And it is self-asserted on BTQ's own blog, not third-party verified.

3. OVERSTATES WHAT WAS DEMONSTRATED. The brief implies a completed STARK verification. The page evidences a Cairo implementation plus a "Cairo profile"; it never states a proof was generated or settled. "Cairo program written and profiled" is what is actually supported.

4. THE BROAD "FIRST" IS FALSE AND WE SHOULD SAY SO. ePrint 2021/1048 (2021-08-16) already verified post-quantum signatures inside a STARK, with benchmarks. BTQ's sentence, read literally without the "on Starknet" qualifier, is contradicted by prior art we have already verified. Our copy should credit 2021/1048 as the earliest STARK PQ-signature verification, and credit BTQ 2023 only as the earliest Falcon-in-Cairo/Starknet work.

UNDERSTATES nothing. One thing the brief omits entirely: BTQ publishes ZERO prover-cost numbers, which actually strengthens our positioning — the cell we are filling is not just empty for ML-DSA, it is empty for Falcon-in-STARK prover cost too.
citation_line: - BTQ, "Completing the First Falcon Signature Verification in Starkware - Initiating the Transition to a Quantum-Safe Ethereum", published **Sep 18, 2023** (accessed 2026-07-23) — [btq.com](https://www.btq.com/blog/completing-the-first-falcon-signature-verification-in-starkware-initiating-the-transition-to-a-quantum-safe-ethereum). Vendor blog. Self-asserts "the first successful STARK-based verification of post-quantum digital signatures **on Starknet**"; Falcon parameter set not stated; describes a Cairo implementation + "Cairo profile" without stating a proof was generated. **No proving time, step count, constraint count, RAM, or proof size published.** The unqualified "first PQ signature in a STARK" reading is contradicted by IACR ePrint 2021/1048 (received 2021-08-16), which benchmarks hash-based PQ signatures inside a STARK two years earlier.
followups:
1. https://www.s2morrow.xyz/ (@feltroidPrime, code at https://github.com/feltroidprime/s2morrow) — an independent Falcon-512-on-Starknet account-abstraction demo that DOES publish gas/calldata figures ("~9.5M L2 gas", "17x calldata compression", "65% less gas than secp256r1 syscall"). This is a better prior-art citation than BTQ for anything quantitative on Falcon-in-Cairo. NEEDS a date check and a repo check before we cite it — no publication date was visible to me, and it publishes no Cairo step count or prover wall-clock either.
2. arXiv 2512.13333, "Quantum Disruption: An SOK of How Post-Quantum Attackers Reshape Blockchain Security and Performance" (https://arxiv.org/html/2512.13333v1) — repeats BTQ's "first" claim as fact and cites no earlier STARK/SNARK PQ-signature work. Useful as evidence that the field's own survey literature has the prior-art gap we are filling; NOT usable as independent confirmation of the "first".
3. StarkWare published a Starknet post-quantum roadmap around 2026-06-30 (thequantuminsider.com/2026/06/30/..., theblock.co/post/406677). Worth verifying separately — if StarkWare has since published concrete PQ prover figures, it changes what "empty cell" we can claim.
4. Aardal et al., compact aggregation of Falcon signatures via LaBRADOR (CRYPTO 2024) — LATER than BTQ and a lattice SNARK rather than a STARK/zkVM, so it does not threaten the ordering, but it is the strongest adjacent Falcon-in-proof-system result and should be in LITERATURE.md so we are not accused of missing it.
5. "Scalable Batch Verification for Post-Quantum Hash-Based Signatures Using STARKs" (SBC / sol.sbc.org.br, appears 2025) — a later Winterfell-based batch verifier reporting "under 3.1 seconds for N = 64" and "50-75 KiB" proofs. Directly overlaps our batch-size axis for hash-based schemes; verify its numbers before we present any batch-vs-cost curve as novel.

HEADLINE: NO. They have NOT published proving numbers. The competitive gap is still open. But there is a name-collision fork that HAS published numbers of a different kind, and it will be mistaken for them.

1) EXISTS, SEMI-DORMANT. github.com/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow, description "STARK-based signature aggregation for Falcon and SPHINCS+". Created 2025-04-27T17:35:58Z. Default branch is `master`, NOT `main` (raw.githubusercontent.com/.../main/README.md returns HTTP 404 — use `master`). 21 commits, 16 stars, 7 forks. Last commit on master: 831bb518b06df614462e8883e1e2c5d627ec437e "fix blake2s hasher (#7)", authored 2026-03-23T11:39:00Z by Michael Zaikin <michael.zaikin@starkware.co>. That is 4 months stale as of access 2026-07-23. Preceding commits: 2025-12-19 (#5), 2025-12-11 (#4 "Update benchmark scripts"), 2025-06-08 (#3), 2025-05-21 (#2). Two OPEN DRAFT PRs, both stale since 2026-02-03: #9 "SPHINCS+ Blake2s variant signature generator", #8 "Add sphincs+ sha2 signature generator". Branches include `bench`, `m-kus/ml-kem`.

2) SCHEMES, verbatim README roadmap (fetched via GitHub API, master, README.md SHA ba24bb845f0ea2744079d5741149d6fe19ca8ca0):
"- [x] Falcon512 verification
- [x] Sphincs+ 128s with SHA2 (simple mode) verification
- [ ] Stwo proving benchmarks

Follow-up:
- [x] Sphincs+ 128s with Blake2s and 4-byte aligned address encoding
- [ ] Falcon512 with probabilistic polynomial multiplication checking"
COMPLETENESS CAVEAT — the Blake2s variant was silently BROKEN for ~3.5 months. Issue #6 (opened 2026-02-02 by @scaraven, closed 2026-03-23): "There is a bug in the blake2s hashing functionality... The `blake2s` hasher ignores most hash operations, and only hashes the suffixes of input messages... this means that the Cairo package incorrectly verifies SPHINCS+ signatures and actually ignores most of the hashing operations." Root cause: `blake2s_compress(state.h, ...)` return value discarded. Consequence stated by the reporter: "incorrect public key output and a massive drop in the number of operations." Any Blake2s cost figure measured between 2025-12-19 and 2026-03-23 would be drastically UNDERSTATED. Their own benchmark scripts predate the fix.

3) STILL UNCHECKED. `- [ ] Stwo proving benchmarks` is unchecked on master AND on the `bench` branch (README byte-identical, same blob SHA ba24bb8). No numbers have been published since the brief was written.

4) PERFORMANCE FIGURES PUBLISHED: NONE by StarkWare. The repo contains only proving *infrastructure*, no results. `bench` branch head d096ef283cbac6631d1299bc31cf4e9af6353d81 "Update benchmarks" (2025-12-11T17:04:12Z) adds `prover_params.json`, `proving_task.json` x2, and a 297,715-line `resources/simple_bootloader_compiled.json` — and ZERO results files. The only numbers in the whole repo are prover config, verbatim from prover_params.json: `"channel_hash": "blake2s"`, `"pow_bits": 26`, `"log_last_layer_degree_bound": 0`, `"log_blowup_factor": 1`, `"n_queries": 70`, `"preprocessed_trace": "canonical_without_pedersen"`. Makefile targets `falcon-execute`/`sphincs-execute` use `--print-resource-usage` and `falcon-args` generates `--num_signatures 1` (single signature; no batch-size sweep is wired up at all). No StarkWare blog post, no delvingbitcoin post with numbers — the delvingbitcoin thread they cite (Ethan Heilman, 2025-04-07) has no measurements either, only "back of the envelope" tx-size estimates (~76 bytes, ~87 tx/s, ~12 bytes, ~555 tx/s) and never mentions s2morrow, Falcon512, SPHINCS+, or Stwo.

5) LICENSE: MIT, "Copyright (c) 2025 Michael Zaikin". Freely citable and derivable. BUT REPRODUCTION IS BLOCKED: the Makefile install target is `cargo +nightly-2025-07-14 install --git ssh://git@github.com/m-kus/proving-utils.git --rev efbaeebfdce3463aa61e16d7d8e6069f03df0994 stwo_run_and_prove --force` — an SSH-only URL to a repo that does not resolve publicly. Every `*-prove` target depends on that binary. So the Cairo verifier code is reproducible; their proving pipeline is NOT, off-the-shelf.

6) MAINTAINER / OFFICIAL: Single maintainer Michael Zaikin (GitHub @m-kus, id 44951260), committing from michael.zaikin@starkware.co, in the `starkware-bitcoin` org (18 repos: raito, shinigami, mikan, stark-symphony, falcon-air...). So: OFFICIAL StarkWare Bitcoin-team research effort, but a one-person exploratory project, not a funded product line.

ADDITIONAL FINDING — NAME COLLISION THAT WILL BE MISREAD AS THEIRS. github.com/feltroidprime/s2morrow is a FORK of starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow, ~208 commits, MIT, last commit 4eff9ab9 2026-03-05T15:18:40Z, with a live marketing site www.s2morrow.xyz ("Falcon-512 | Post-Quantum Signatures on Starknet", "Built by @feltroidPrime"). It HAS published numbers, but they are Cairo-VM STEP COUNTS and STARKNET L2 GAS for on-chain verification — NOT STARK prover time, NOT batched, NOT amortized. Verbatim from its README: "Profiled at commit `a1f5ed9` with `cairo-profiler` (cumulative steps): `verify` (e2e) | 63,177 ... `verify_with_msg_point` | 26,301 ... `hash_to_point` | 5,988 ... Total test cost: 119,034 steps (includes deserialization overhead). ... L2 gas: `verify` ~13.2M | `verify_with_msg_point` ~56.6M". Site figures: "~9.5M L2 gas" Falcon-512, "~27.4M" secp256r1 syscall, "~4.6M" secp256r1 garaga, "~16.6M" secp256k1 syscall, "~4.5M" secp256k1 garaga, "65% savings", "17x calldata compression", "1,030 felts reduced to 62 felts on-chain", "512 Zq coefficients packed into 29 felt252 storage slots", "2x" garaga ECDSA. NOTE the site says ~9.5M where its own README says ~13.2M for `verify` — those two published figures do not agree, and no date/commit is given for the site number. The fork also dropped SPHINCS+ and aggregation entirely and swapped SHAKE-256 for Poseidon in hash-to-point, so it is not comparable to any FIPS-conformant benchmark.

ALSO RELEVANT: github.com/starkware-bitcoin/falcon-air — a separate StarkWare Bitcoin repo, custom Stwo AIR for Z_q (q=12289) Falcon arithmetic with NTT/INTT circuits, created 2025-07-17, last updated 2025-11-27, 2 stars. README self-describes: "Research prototype. This code has not been audited." It publishes NO performance numbers and has NO LICENSE file (its README says "If you intend this project to be MIT-licensed, add a LICENSE file"), so it is legally unsafe to vendor.

discrepancies:
The brief is ACCURATE on every point I could test — repo exists, Falcon-512 + SPHINCS+ in Cairo/Stwo, goal is proving-time comparison and benchmarking vs LaBRADOR, and "Stwo proving benchmarks" is UNCHECKED as of 2026-07-23. Three refinements, none contradicting it:
(a) UNDERSTATES completeness — the brief omits a THIRD completed item, "[x] Sphincs+ 128s with Blake2s and 4-byte aligned address encoding", and omits that this variant was provably broken (issue #6) from 2025-12-19 until the 2026-03-23 fix.
(b) The scheme is "Sphincs+ 128s with SHA2 (simple mode)", i.e. SPHINCS+-128s-simple specifically, not SPHINCS+ generally, and Falcon is "Falcon512". Cite the parameter sets, not the family names.
(c) The brief says "as of July 2026" — precisely, the repo has been untouched since 2026-03-23, so "unchecked as of July 2026" is true but the state actually froze in March.
LOUD PROCESS DISCONFIRMATION: WebFetch's page-summarizer reading github.com/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow returned "**Completed:** ... ✅ Stwo proving benchmarks". That is FALSE — the raw README says `- [ ] Stwo proving benchmarks`. The summarizer also silently mis-sorted the checked follow-up item into "Planned". If anyone on this project sources roadmap state from a WebFetch summary rather than the raw file, they will publish a wrong claim about our closest competitor. Always pull README.md through the GitHub API / raw blob, and note that raw `.../main/README.md` 404s here because the default branch is `master`.
SECOND DISCONFIRMATION: the search-surfaced URL docs.swmansion.com/maat/nightly-nightly-2026-04-18-nightly-2026-04-19/keep-starknet-strange/s2morrow-831bb518b/logs.txt — a third-party CI run against the exact s2morrow master commit 831bb518b, which would have contained real resource-usage output — returns HTTP 404. The nightly directory has rotated. Do not cite it.
THIRD, the trap: web search conflates starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow with feltroidprime/s2morrow + s2morrow.xyz and will hand you "~9.5M L2 gas" as if StarkWare published it. It is a fork, by a different author, measuring on-chain Starknet gas and Cairo VM steps for ONE signature with Poseidon (not SHAKE-256) hash-to-point and no SPHINCS+ and no aggregation. It is NOT a prover-cost benchmark and is NOT FIPS-conformant. And the fork's own two published Falcon-512 `verify` gas figures disagree with each other: README "~13.2M" vs site "~9.5M".
followups:
1. THE CELL IS STILL EMPTY, AND IT IS WIDER THAN WE THOUGHT. Our closest competitor has been frozen for 4 months with its single benchmark deliverable unchecked, its batch-size sweep never wired up (`--num_signatures 1` only), and its proving toolchain behind a private SSH dependency. Nobody has published amortized prover cost per PQ signature vs batch size in Cairo/Stwo either.
2. REPRODUCIBILITY BLOCKER TO RECORD IN LITERATURE.md: `ssh://git@github.com/m-kus/proving-utils.git` rev efbaeeb is not publicly resolvable, so `make falcon-prove` / `make sphincs-prove` cannot be run by a third party as-is. If we want a Cairo/Stwo column in PQ-STARK-BENCH we must either get that repo opened or replace it with stwo-cairo's own runner. Worth an issue on their repo — a friendly, citable, public-record ask.
3. THEIR CAIRO VERIFIER CODE IS MIT AND VENDORABLE. Falcon512 and SPHINCS+-128s (SHA2 + Blake2s) verifiers in Cairo, Copyright (c) 2025 Michael Zaikin. We can legitimately cite and even reuse it for a Cairo/Stwo lane. falcon-air, by contrast, has NO LICENSE FILE — do not vendor it.
4. ADJACENT, UNREPORTED: branch `m-kus/ml-kem` (sha 7e73c8a) suggests ML-KEM work in Cairo. Unmerged, undocumented, but it is a signal about where they go next.
5. NEW COMPETITOR CLASS: starkware-bitcoin/falcon-air is a HAND-WRITTEN Stwo AIR for Falcon (Z_q arithmetic + NTT/INTT + range checks in one "Big AIR"), i.e. the custom-circuit alternative to the zkVM route. It publishes no numbers and is unlicensed and stale (2025-11-27), but its existence means the honest framing of our benchmark is "general-purpose zkVM lane vs custom-AIR lane", and we should say so rather than imply zkVM is the only approach.
6. STRENGTHENS THE ROUND-1 ASYMMETRY RULE. The fork swapped SHAKE-256 for Poseidon precisely because the native hash is cheap in its proof system. That is the same class of distortion as RISC Zero/SP1 accelerating Ed25519 but not ML-DSA. Every chart needs the hash-primitive and precompile asymmetry disclosed, or a reviewer will correctly call the comparison rigged.
7. Ecosystem context worth a separate verification pass: StarkWare announced a Starknet post-quantum roadmap (covered by The Block and ForkLog, both surfaced in search on 2026-07-23, neither fetched by me). If that roadmap contains prover numbers it would supersede this finding — check it before publishing.

what_the_source_actually_says:
The landing page LOADS (https://dilithium-zk-landing.vercel.app/, accessed 2026-07-23). It is a marketing landing page, title "Dilithium ZK: Post-Quantum Signatures in Zero Knowledge". It claims verbatim: "Proof generation time of 22 seconds"; "Proof size of 260 bytes for on-chain verification"; "104 tests passed with 132,000+ fuzzing iterations"; "Approximate cost per proof at $0.045"; "260-byte Groth16 proofs verified on any EVM chain"; NTT "~1.3 µs forward and ~1.9 µs inverse", "verified NTT ... ~5.1 µs with 60-bit soundness". It points to `sp1-ntt-gadget = "0.1"` on crates.io and to github.com/kota1026/pq-wallet-sp1-ntt-gadget-.

LEVEL: It is ML-DSA-65 (NIST Level 3), NOT ML-DSA-44. crates.io description verbatim: "NTT/INTT Custom Gadget for Dilithium (ML-DSA-65) verification in SP1 zkVM with 60-bit soundness PIC". docs.rs lists Prime Q = 8,380,417, N = 256, NUM_CHALLENGES = 4.

SCOPE (the decisive finding): the published artifact is NOT an ML-DSA verifier. The crate's own README (https://docs.rs/crate/sp1-ntt-gadget/0.1.0/source/README.md) states it is the NTT/INTT gadget with Polynomial Identity Check only and is "not a complete ML-DSA signature verifier". The Medium writeup's future-work section likewise lists "Further optimization (target: under 10 seconds)" and "Extend to other PQC signatures (Falcon, SLH-DSA)" as incomplete.

22s FIGURE: writeup (https://medium.com/@phillyj1026/..., dated December 8, 2025, author "Kota") states "Proof time: 22.07 seconds", "Proof size: 260 bytes", mode Groth16, achieved on "SP1 Network". SP1 Network is Succinct's REMOTE/outsourced proving service — so NO local machine, CPU model, GPU, or core count is published for the 22.07 s. Hardware is not published. The only named hardware is Apple M1, and that is for the NATIVE (non-zkVM) microbenchmarks: "Native (Apple M1 hardware): Forward NTT ~1.3 µs, Inverse NTT ~1.9 µs, Verified NTT ~5.1 µs". Those µs figures are NOT proving times.

260 BYTES: yes, this is a Groth16 wrapper proof for EVM verification — README "SP1 Network Proof (Groth16): Proof size: 260 bytes ... On-chain verifiable: Yes". Note 260 bytes is the standard SP1 Groth16 on-chain proof-bytes size, not a property specific to this work.

SP1 VERSION: not stated in the blog or landing page, but pinned in the crate manifest (https://docs.rs/crate/sp1-ntt-gadget/0.1.0/source/Cargo.toml.orig): `sp1-zkvm = "5.0"` under `[target.'cfg(target_os = "zkvm")'.dependencies]` and `sp1-sdk = { version = "5.2.3", features = ["network"] }` as a dev-dependency. So SP1 v5.x, NOT the v6.3.1 line.

discrepancies:
Multiple, and they are material.

1. SCOPE OVERSTATEMENT (biggest). The claim as written — "ML-DSA-65 verification in SP1 with NTT gadget; published ~22s proof" — implies a full ML-DSA-65 signature verification was proven. The crate's own README says it is "not a complete ML-DSA signature verifier"; it ships only NTT/INTT + a Polynomial Identity Check. So the 22.07 s cannot be cited as "cost to verify an ML-DSA-65 signature in SP1". What program the 22.07 s actually proved is never stated unambiguously.

2. THE CYCLE ARITHMETIC DOES NOT CLOSE. The published breakdown sums to 580,000 + 200,000 + 320,000 = 1,100,000 cycles, but the stated "Total" is 5,625,411 cycles — off by 5.1x. Separately, 580,000 is labelled "~50%" of the total, yet 580,000 / 5,625,411 = 10.3%. The percentages (50/18/32) are internally consistent only against a ~1.1M total, contradicting the stated 5,625,411. Either the total or the breakdown is wrong; both are published side by side. This is the same self-inconsistency flagged in round 1 — now pinned to exact figures.

3. "260-byte on-chain verification" is not a result of this work. It is the fixed SP1 Groth16 on-chain proof size; any SP1 program wrapped in Groth16 emits it. Presenting it as a project metric is misleading framing, though not factually false.

4. HARDWARE IS NOT PUBLISHED. 22.07 s is on "SP1 Network" — an outsourced proving service with undisclosed and time-varying hardware. It is not a machine-pinned measurement and is not comparable to a local-prover number. The only named hardware (Apple M1) attaches to native µs microbenchmarks that are not proving times; conflating the two would be a serious error.

5. REPRODUCIBILITY IS BROKEN AT THE LINK LEVEL. Every public surface points at a GitHub repo that has never received a commit (size 0, no branches, pushed_at == created_at). Partial source survives only inside the crates.io tarball, and the manifest's `exclude` strips tests/, examples/, and scripts/ — so the 104 tests, the 132,731 fuzz iterations, and the two named benchmark examples (prove_single, prove_benchmark) that generated every headline number are in NO public artifact. The test/fuzz counts are unverifiable claims.

6. "$0.045 per proof" has no published methodology, no SP1 Network price sheet reference, and no date on the pricing.

7. Minor: docs.rs shows "Release Date: June 13, 2026", which is a docs rebuild, not the crate release (2025-12-08). Do not cite the June date.

what_the_source_actually_says:
THE SITE LOADS. It is maintained by Thom Wiggers / PQShield (footer: "Built by Thom Wiggers / PQShield"), repo github.com/PQShield/nist-sigs-zoo, CC BY-SA 4.0, and it is ACTIVELY maintained (last commit 2026-07-22, one day before access). It covers 116 parameter sets across Lattices, Multivariate, Symmetric, Isogenies, MPCitH and Pre-Quantum families.

IT DOES PUBLISH TIMING NUMBERS, not just sizes. Table column headers: "Scheme", "Category", "Status", "Parameter Set", "Level", "pk (B)", "sig (B)", "pk+sig (B)", "Sign", "Verify", "Assumption". The underlying YAML carries four numeric perf fields per parameter set: signing_cycles, verification_cycles, signing_us, verification_us.

BUT THERE IS NO KEYGEN COLUMN AND NO KEYGEN FIELD anywhere in the signature dataset. The claim's "keygen numbers" is wrong for the signatures page. (Keygen exists only on the separate KEMs page, which reports keygen/encaps/decaps.)

PROVENANCE IS HETEROGENEOUS AND THIS IS THE CENTRAL PROBLEM. README: "Parameter sizes and performance timings (up to and including round 2) are copied from the individual scheme submission documents - be aware of potential errors. From round 3 on we do our own benchmarking." Each scheme YAML carries its own perf_source. For the six schemes we care about: ML-DSA perf_source is "OQS bench"; Falcon perf_source is "OQS bench"; SLH-DSA perf_source is "submission document"; EdDSA perf_source is "OpenSSL bench"; ECDSA.yaml has NO perf_source field at all (provenance undocumented).

The site's own disclosed benchmark environment (data/benchmark_env.yaml) is: cpu model "12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-12650H", 8 cores, 2 threads_per_core, max_freq_mhz 4600, governor powersave, turbo enabled; os "Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie)"; kernel "6.19.11-zabbly+"; compiler "cc (Debian 14.2.0-19) 14.2.0"; openssl "OpenSSL 3.6.2 7 Apr 2026"; notes "Median over 1000 iterations (fewer for slow schemes). Cycle counter: rdpmc CPU_CYCLES (real core cycles, user-space). Wall clock: clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC). Benchmark thread pinned to one core." THAT ENVIRONMENT DOES NOT GOVERN THE ROWS WE NEED - those rows are attributed to OQS bench / submission doc / OpenSSL / nothing.

IT IS x86-ONLY. No aarch64, no Apple Silicon, anywhere in the dataset.

NO secp256k1. ECDSA.yaml contains only P-256, P-384, P-521. For the Bitcoin/Ethereum curve the zoo gives us nothing.

CROSS-SOURCE CHECK, eBACS/SUPERCOP (https://bench.cr.yp.to/results-sign.html, page version "20260717 22:08:42", accessed 2026-07-23): loads, and DOES have aarch64 machines - "2023 Broadcom BCM2712; 4 x 1500MHz; pi5", "2019 Broadcom BCM2711; 4 x 1500MHz; pi4b", "2018 Broadcom BCM2837B0; 4 x 1400MHz; pi3aplus". No Apple Silicon. Has ed25519, dilithium2, falcon512dyn/falcon512tree, sphincs256. No secp256k1 ECDSA. Note dilithium2/falcon512 there are round-3 submissions, NOT the final FIPS 204/206 parameterizations.

pq-crystals official page (https://pq-crystals.org/dilithium/index.shtml, accessed 2026-07-23) publishes Dilithium2 AVX2 on "Intel Core-i7 6600U (Skylake)": gen 124031, sign 333013, verify 118412 cycles, pk 1312 B, sig 2420 B.

discrepancies:
THE BRIEF OVERSTATES THIS SOURCE ON FOUR COUNTS, and one of them is disqualifying for its intended use.

1. "keygen numbers" - FALSE for the signature dataset. There is no keygen column and no keygen field in any signature YAML. Only sign and verify. Keygen appears only on the separate KEMs page.

2. "usable as a baseline cross-check for our native benchmarks" - ONLY AS AN ORDER-OF-MAGNITUDE SANITY CHECK, NOT AS A PRECISION BASELINE. The timings are not one uniform measurement campaign. The site discloses a single benchmark environment (i7-12650H, Debian 13) but that environment covers the round-3 on-ramp schemes, NOT our six targets. Our six rows are attributed to four different provenances: "OQS bench" (ML-DSA, Falcon), "submission document" (SLH-DSA), "OpenSSL bench" (EdDSA), and nothing at all (ECDSA). My own derived implied-clock check confirms this: cycles/us across rows implies clocks from 2187 MHz to 3492 MHz, i.e. these rows cannot have come from one pinned machine. Anyone citing a single "the zoo says X" number across schemes is comparing measurements taken on different machines with different compilers.

3. "extensive... across schemes" - true in breadth (116 parameter sets) but NOT in the dimensions we need. It is x86-ONLY: no aarch64, no Apple Silicon anywhere. Since our harness targets Apple Silicon aarch64, THIS SOURCE CANNOT VALIDATE OUR NATIVE NUMBERS AT ALL on the platform we are actually running.

4. secp256k1 IS ABSENT. ECDSA.yaml has only P-256/P-384/P-521. Our benchmark needs the Bitcoin/Ethereum curve. The zoo contributes zero on that row.

DISCONFIRMATIONS FOUND, FLAGGING LOUDLY:

A. https://pqshield.github.io/nist-sigs-zoo/wide.html RETURNS HTTP 404. It is still surfaced by search engines (titled "Post-Quantum signatures zoo"). Do not cite it. The live site is a SvelteKit app; the canonical citable artifacts are the repo YAMLs, not any /wide.html.

B. TWO PUBLISHED, MUTUALLY INCONSISTENT ML-DSA-44 NUMBERS, BOTH FROM CREDIBLE SOURCES, DIFFERING BY ~1.9x. The zoo's current data/schemes/ML-DSA.yaml says signing_cycles 172926, verification_cycles 76028. pq-crystals' own official page says sign 333013, verify 118412 (AVX2, Skylake i7-6600U). The zoo's OWN legacy file static/data/parametersets.csv still carries the pq-crystals values verbatim ("ML-DSA,ML-DSA-44,2,1312,2420,333013,118412,,"). So the zoo silently reparented ML-DSA from pq-crystals-on-Skylake to OQS-bench and the stale CSV was never removed. If our harness lands anywhere in 170k-340k cycles for ML-DSA-44 sign, that is NOT evidence our harness is broken - the "baseline" itself spans that range.

C. THE Ed25519 AND ECDSA P-256 ROWS ARE SUSPICIOUSLY SLOW AND WILL GENERATE A FALSE ALARM AGAINST OUR HARNESS. Zoo Ed25519 sign is 126662 cycles / 42.0 us; well-optimized Ed25519 (ref10/donna/ed25519-dalek class) is roughly a third of that. Zoo ECDSA P-256 verify is 235386 cycles / 107.2 us, which is several times slower than OpenSSL builds with nistz256 assembly. Both rows are labelled "OpenSSL bench" (EdDSA) or unlabelled (ECDSA). If our Rust harness reports Ed25519 sign 2-3x FASTER than this table, our harness is probably RIGHT and the table is measuring a slow generic build. Do not "correct" our harness toward these numbers.

D. INTERNAL INCONSISTENCY IN THE REPO. data/benchmark_env.yaml lists submodule sources for mldsa (pq-crystals/dilithium), slhdsa (pq-code-package/slhdsa-c) and fndsa (pornin/c-fn-dsa), which implies those three WERE measured on the i7-12650H; but the corresponding scheme YAMLs attribute their numbers to "OQS bench" and "submission document". One of the two is stale. Provenance for exactly our target rows is therefore not reliably established, and I could not resolve which is authoritative.

E. The legacy static/data/parametersets.csv Ed25519 row is internally incoherent: "EdDSA,Ed25519,Pre-Quantum,32,64,42000,130000,0.00274,8.0" - 42000 cycles paired with 0.00274 ms implies a 15 GHz CPU, and 130000 cycles paired with 8.0 ms implies 16 MHz. That file is broken; use data/schemes/*.yaml only.

F. eBACS has aarch64 but only Raspberry Pi class (Cortex-A53/A72/A76). NO Apple Silicon on eBACS either. And its "dilithium2"/"falcon512" entries are ROUND-3 submissions, not FIPS 204 / FIPS 206 final parameterizations - not interchangeable with ML-DSA-44.

G. I could NOT extract the timing tables from arXiv 2601.17785 ("Performance Analysis of Quantum-Secure Digital Signature Algorithms in Blockchain", Tushar Jain, dated 2026-01-27). The PDF's compressed streams defeated text extraction and NO hardware/CPU was recoverable. Treat as UNVERIFIED - do not cite its numbers until someone opens the PDF properly.

H. NO PUBLISHED ABSOLUTE secp256k1 VERIFY NUMBER ON aarch64 WAS FOUND ANYWHERE. The delvingbitcoin thread's arm64 run publishes only a relative bar chart with no CPU model and no units. Web-search snippets floating "47.6 microseconds" and "88.70 microseconds" for libsecp256k1 verify trace to Medium/aggregator pages I did NOT fetch and did NOT verify - I am explicitly NOT asserting them. For secp256k1 we will have to measure and publish our own native baseline; there is no citable one for our platform.

NET: cite nist-sigs-zoo for SIZES (pk/sig bytes - those are spec-derived and solid) and for coarse ORDER-OF-MAGNITUDE sign/verify ranking. Do NOT cite it as a precision timing baseline, do NOT cite it for keygen, and do NOT use it to validate anything measured on Apple Silicon.

THE SUSPECTED ID ERROR DOES NOT EXIST. Both arXiv IDs resolve, to two DIFFERENT real papers, both submitted 9 Mar 2026. Neither is a typo for the other.

=== arXiv 2603.07947 = "Lattice" (the claim's paper) — CONFIRMED ===
https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07947 resolves. Title: "Lattice: A Post-Quantum Settlement Layer" (full running title on p.1: "A Quantum-Resistant, Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System with Per-Block Difficulty Adjustment and Perpetual Tail Emission"). Single author: David Alejandro Trejo Pizzo. Submitted "[v1] Mon, 9 Mar 2026 04:30:04 UTC (50 KB)", v1 only. Comments field: "Version 0.8.0, 3 figures". Primary category quant-ph, cross-list cs.CR. No journal ref. Accessed 2026-07-23.

Abstract verbatim (excerpt): "Lattice combines three independent defense vectors: hardware resilience through RandomX CPU-only proof-of-work, network resilience through LWMA-1 per-block difficulty adjustment ..., and cryptographic resilience through ML-DSA-44 post-quantum digital signatures (NIST FIPS 204, lattice-based), enforced exclusively from the genesis block with no classical signature fallback."

https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07974 resolves. Title: "ZK-ACE: Identity-Centric Zero-Knowledge Authorization for Post-Quantum Blockchain Systems". Author: Jian Sheng Wang. "Submitted on 9 Mar 2026 (v1); last revised 28 May 2026 (v3)". Accessed 2026-07-23.

Table 7 verbatim, extracted from the v3 PDF. Caption: "Table 7: Circle STARK batch prove/verify timings and proof sizes (single-threaded, Apple Silicon, Criterion.rs medians, zk-ace v0.4.0). Proof size is the serialized ACBP batch proof; amortized size divides by N."

  1  |  9 |  14.29 ms | 14.29 ms |  3.18 ms | 3.18 ms | 122 KB | 122 KB

Methodology verbatim (§12.2): "All measurements were collected using Criterion.rs (100 samples per benchmark) on Apple Silicon (M-series), single-threaded execution, release-mode compiler optimizations (--release). These figures should be interpreted as reference measurements for the reported implementation and hardware; a public artifact should identify the repository URL, commit hash, Rust toolchain, benchmark commands, and machine configuration used to reproduce them."
Supporting prose (§12.2): "Batch prove time scales near-linearly with N because trace generation dominates and is O(N); the FRI commitment layer adds a O(log N) overhead that is small in practice." "batch verification rises from 3.18 ms at N = 1 to 37.97 ms at N = 16, or about 2.37 ms per transaction." Table 8: "Constraints 341 active rows / 361 AIR expr. | 2,155 R1CS"; "Prove time 14.5 ms | 37.3 ms"; "Verify time 1.1 ms | 1.6 ms"; "Proof size ~107 KB | 128 B". On-chain model: "Per-transaction consensus-visible data consists of the five public input fields — approximately 160 bytes — plus an amortized share of the aggregated proof submitted once per block."

Notably, ZK-ACE's §12.2 independently corroborates the established round-1 finding that prover cost does NOT amortize: prove/tx is flat at 14.29-15.76 ms across N=1..16 while proof/tx falls 122 KB -> 13 KB.

discrepancies:
ON THE ARXIV IDs: NONE. The suspected transposition is a false alarm — do NOT "correct" either ID. 2603.07947 = Lattice, 2603.07974 = ZK-ACE, both real, both submitted 9 Mar 2026. Every element of the claim (title "Lattice", ID 2603.07947, March 2026, PQ-only chain, ML-DSA-44 posted naively on-chain, no STARK layer, native perf + tx-size analysis present) checks out verbatim.

FOUR THINGS TO FIX BEFORE PUBLISHING, though:

1. HARDWARE OVERSTATED FOR ZK-ACE TABLE 7. The brief says "Apple M3 Pro". Tables 5, 6 and 7 all say only "single-threaded, Apple Silicon, Criterion.rs medians"; §12.2 says "on Apple Silicon (M-series)". "Apple M3 Pro" appears ONLY in the Table 12 caption ("ACE runtime pipeline benchmarks (single-threaded, Apple M3 Pro)"). Attributing Table 7 to an M3 Pro is a reasonable inference but is NOT what the paper states. Cite Table 7's hardware as "Apple Silicon (M-series), single-threaded" and footnote the M3 Pro as inferred from Table 12.

2. THE "14.29 to 14.77 ms/tx" RANGE IS WRONG — it silently drops the maximum. Prove/tx is NOT monotone: 14.29, 14.45, 14.46, 15.76 (N=8), 14.77 (N=16). The true range is 14.29 to 15.76 ms/tx, and the peak is at N=8, not N=16. Quoting "14.29 to 14.77" understates the spread and hides a non-monotonicity the paper never explains. Also, N is 1,2,4,8,16 (five points), not "N=1..16" (sixteen points).

3. ZK-ACE's OWN CAVEAT SHOULD TRAVEL WITH THE NUMBERS. §12.2 explicitly says these "should be interpreted as reference measurements for the reported implementation and hardware; a public artifact should identify the repository URL, commit hash, Rust toolchain, benchmark commands, and machine configuration used to reproduce them" — i.e. the authors themselves flag that no reproducible artifact accompanies these figures. §12.4 lists "Single-platform benchmarks" as a stated limitation. Treat Table 7 as published-but-unreproduced, not as an independently verified benchmark.

4. LATTICE HAS AN INTERNAL CONTRADICTION IN ITS OWN VERIFY NUMBERS — do not cite it as a clean ECDSA baseline. §3.2's table gives ECDSA verification as "~0.15 ms" (= ~6,700 verify/s) and concludes "ML-DSA-44 verification is actually faster than ECDSA verification". But §9 gives "ECDSA's ~40,000-70,000 signatures/second" (= ~0.014-0.025 ms) and says ML-DSA-44 is "approximately 2-3x slower", and §3.4.3 gives yet a third pair, "~40K/s" vs "~20K/s (2x slower)". The §3.2 ECDSA figure is off by roughly 6-10x from the paper's own §9 figure, and the two sections reach OPPOSITE conclusions about which scheme verifies faster. ZK-ACE independently reports "secp256k1 ECDSA verification on the same hardware takes approximately 50--100 us", consistent with Lattice's §9 and inconsistent with its §3.2. If you use Lattice's ML-DSA-44 native verify time (~0.05 ms), cite it alone and take the ECDSA baseline from elsewhere.

ALSO DISCONFIRMED: Lattice's §"Installation" lists "git clone https://github.com/lattice-network/lattice.git". That repository returns HTTP 404 as of 2026-07-23. No code is publicly available at the URL the paper publishes, so every Lattice number here is author-claimed and unreproducible.

PROVENANCE CAVEATS: Lattice is a single-author v1 preprint, filed with quant-ph as the PRIMARY category (cs.CR only cross-listed) — an odd choice for a consensus-protocol paper — self-labeled "Version 0.8.0", with no journal reference and no peer review. It is a whitepaper, not a measured systems paper. ZK-ACE is likewise single-author with no journal reference, though it has reached v3.

followups:
1. ZK-ACE is a strong prior-art hazard for PQ-STARK-BENCH's framing, but note WHAT it actually measures: it does NOT verify an ML-DSA/Falcon signature in-circuit. Its whole thesis is avoiding that — "replaces transaction-carried signature objects with identity-bound ZK statements ... no signature object is produced or verified on-chain", with a "deterministic identity derivation primitive (DIDP) as a black box". So its 14.5 ms is the cost of a Poseidon2/identity-recovery circuit, NOT of PQ signature verification. Its own abstract says the alternative is "roughly 500--2,300x" more expensive, and §12.2 admits "Verifying them inside ZK circuits merely relocates the cost via expensive lattice arithmetic in prover circuits" — i.e. ZK-ACE explicitly declines to measure the exact cell PQ-STARK-BENCH is trying to fill. That is good news for the benchmark's novelty and should be stated plainly in LITERATURE.md, because a reviewer who only reads Table 7 will assume it is the same measurement.

2. Its DIDP framing deserves adversarial scrutiny. "Deterministic identity derivation primitive ... as a black box" with "DIDP recovery hardness" as an assumption is doing enormous load-bearing work; the order-of-magnitude data reduction is contingent on a primitive the paper does not instantiate. Do not cite ZK-ACE's compression ratios as measured PQ-in-STARK results.

3. The Circle STARK / Stwo backend is a third prover family beyond RISC Zero and SP1, and Table 7 is the closest published analogue to PQ-STARK-BENCH's headline chart. Consider citing it as the methodological template (Criterion medians, batch sweep, prove/tx + proof/tx columns) while noting the workload differs.

4. Lattice supplies a clean, citable NON-ZK baseline for the "what does naive on-chain PQ cost" column: 2,420 B signature + 1,312 B pubkey, ~14.6 tx/s, ~7.4 TB/year. Useful as the left-hand bar in any chart, but flag it as author-claimed with a dead code link.

5. Lattice's §3.2 claim that ML-DSA-44 verification (~0.05 ms) beats ECDSA (~0.15 ms) is worth an independent bench on your own hardware; if true even directionally, it materially changes the PQ-penalty narrative for the NATIVE column, which then makes the zkVM precompile asymmetry (Ed25519/ECDSA accelerated, no lattice/NTT precompile in either RISC Zero or SP1) the entire source of the in-circuit penalty. That is a sharper and more defensible headline than "PQ is slow".

6. Both papers are single-author March-2026 preprints filed the same day with adjacent arXiv IDs. Worth a quick check for other PQ-blockchain preprints in the 2603.079xx range before finalizing prior art.

Note the column is labelled "Signature Size", not "proof size" — in this construction the STARK proof IS the aggregate signature ("This proof then serves the role of the aggregate or threshold signature", abstract). Note also the THRESHOLD rows are n = 127/255/511/1023, not 128/256/512/1024.

HARDWARE — CONFIRMED VERBATIM (Sec. 6, PDF p.11): "We ran benchmarks on an 8-core Intel Core i9 processor @ 2.4 GHz with 32 GB of RAM." Immediately preceding: "Our implementation of STARK prover supports multi-threaded proof generation, but does not make use of any advanced CPU instructions, leaving room for optimizations in future work." (i.e. no SIMD/AVX — relevant to any cross-prover comparison.)

SECURITY LEVELS — CONFIRMED VERBATIM, but they are STARK PROOF security levels, not signature security levels. Table 1 (PDF p.3): "Security level | Base field | Blowup factor | Query count | Grinding factor | Folding factor | Hash function" → "96 bits | 128 bits | 8 | 27 | 16 | 8 | BLAKE3" and "123 bits | 128 bits | 8 | 34 | 22 | 8 | BLAKE3". Caption: "For 96-bit security level, output of BLAKE3 hash function is truncated to 24 bytes to provide up to 96-bit collision resistance. For 123-bit security level, FRI protocol must be run in a quadratic extension of the base field." Sec. 6.2: "We benchmark STARK proof generation at different security levels, by varying the number of queries included in a proof". Separately, the SIGNATURE targets 123 bits: "Our instantiation of plain Lamport+ has 32-byte public keys, produces 8KB signatures, and targets 123 bits of security. We also provide an optimized Lamport+ version applying a 'mining' technique to the message hash to be signed, which allows targeting 127 bits of security."

(1) SCHEME — Lamport+, and it is NOT NIST-standardized. Sec. 3: "Lamport+ is a WOTS instance for 𝑤 = 2 (Winternitz parameter)"; intro: "we present a one-time signature (OTS) scheme, Lamport+ which is described in detail in Sec. 3 and based on Lamport's original one-time signatures. The primary motivation for using Lamport+ is its efficient encoding in ZKP systems." It is instantiated with the Rescue-Prime hash function over a 128-bit prime field q = 2^128 − 45·2^40 + 1, i.e. an arithmetization-friendly hash chosen for the AIR, NOT SHA-2/SHAKE. Table 2 (PDF p.12): "Private key size 16 KB | Public key size 32 bytes | Signature size 8 KB | Signing time 30 ms | Verification time 20 ms", caption "Performance of Lamport+ signature scheme instantiated with Rescue-Prime hash function." No FIPS/NIST standardization claim is made for Lamport+ anywhere; NIST is only referenced for OTHER schemes (XMSS/SP 800-208 in the related-work paragraph, and the NIST PQC round-2 status report as ref [1]). Table 4 lists Falcon-512 and Dilithium3 only as SIZE comparisons ("Falcon-512 | Yes | 897 B | 618 B | 603 KB*", "Dilithium3 | Yes | 1.9 KB | 3.3 KB | 3.2 MB*", where "*" = "Schemes that do not support aggregation ... we assume concatenating individual signatures"). The paper does NOT verify Falcon or Dilithium in-circuit.

(3) AMORTIZED PER-SIGNATURE COST — NOT PUBLISHED. The strings "amortiz", "per signature" and "per-signature" do not appear anywhere in the PDF. The 19.5 / 19.9 / 20.5 / 19.2 ms figures are a DERIVATION (2.5s/128 = 19.53 ms; 5.1s/256 = 19.92 ms; 10.5s/512 = 20.51 ms; 19.7s/1024 = 19.24 ms). The arithmetic checks out against the published Table 3 96-bit column, but it must be labelled "derived by us from Table 3", never "published by the paper".

(5) SCALING CLAIMS — the paper is explicit about VERIFIER-side linearity, and only indirect about prover-side. Sec. 6.2: "Aggregate and threshold signature verification times are between 3 ms and 10 ms. As explained in Sec. 4.3, verification time is linear in the number of individual signatures. However, practical impact of this linear complexity is negligible when verifying an aggregation of fewer than 10, 000 signatures." And: "As Table 3 shows, a large number of signatures may take significant time to aggregate. However, STARK proof generation is massively parallelizable, so prover time may be reduced significantly by utilizing more powerful hardware. For example, aggregating 1024 signatures at 96-bit security level can be completed in 3.9 seconds on a 64-core machine (vs. 19.7 seconds on an 8-core machine)." Sec. 4.3 also notes "complexities 𝑏(𝑥) and the denominator of the constraint are linear in the number of signatures". The paper does NOT contain a sentence asserting prover-time linearity in N — that assertion is stronger in the Winterfell README (see below), and in the paper it is only implicit in Table 3's numbers.

(4) CODE — the paper does NOT link facebook/winterfell. Sec. 1.1: "Moreover, we have implemented a fully-featured and performant STARK prover and verifier in Rust, and open-sourced our implementation under the MIT license [6]. In addition to the STARK prover and verifier, our open-source Github repository contains examples of all AIRs described in this work." Reference [6] reads verbatim: "[6] A. authors. STARK prover and verifier (Rust implementation). https://github.com/anonauthorsub/asiaccs_2021_440." discrepancies:
Four discrepancies, one of them material:

1. MATERIAL — CODE URL IS WRONG. The brief asserts "Code asserted to be in https://github.com/facebook/winterfell". The paper does NOT say that. It cites reference [6] verbatim as "A. authors. STARK prover and verifier (Rust implementation). https://github.com/anonauthorsub/asiaccs_2021_440" — a non-de-anonymized submission URL that returns HTTP 404 today (verified via GitHub API, 2026-07-23). The facebook/winterfell link is an inference from author identity (top contributor irakliyk = "Irakliy Khaburzaniya" <irakliy81@gmail.com>, the exact email on the paper's front page), matching example names (examples/src/lamport/{aggregate,threshold}), matching instantiation (Rescue-Prime, 32-byte pk, 8 KB sig, 123-bit target), matching hardware class and MIT license. GitHub code search across repo:facebook/winterfell for "asiaccs OR Khaburzaniya OR 3524128" returns 0 hits — there is NO cross-citation in either direction. Anywhere we cite the artifact we must write "inferred, not stated by the paper; the paper's own artifact link is dead."

2. AMORTIZED FIGURES ARE OURS, NOT THEIRS. 19.5 / 19.9 / 20.5 / 19.2 ms are arithmetically correct against the published 96-bit aggregate column (19.531 / 19.922 / 20.508 / 19.238 ms respectively), but the paper never computes or prints them; the words "amortized" and "per signature" appear nowhere in the 15-page PDF. Label as derived.

3. "TWO SECURITY LEVELS (96-bit and 123-bit)" IS RIGHT BUT AMBIGUOUS. Those are STARK proof soundness levels set by FRI query count (27 vs 34 queries, Table 1), not the signature's security. Lamport+ itself "targets 123 bits of security" (127 with the mining optimization). A chart labelled "96-bit" without that footnote will read as a weakened signature, which it is not.

4. BATCH SIZES 128/256/512/1024 HOLD ONLY FOR THE AGGREGATE ROWS. The threshold rows are n = 127/255/511/1023 (one less than a power of two, because the Merkle tree of signers has depth one less than a power of 2). Do not present them as the same x-axis points.

Also worth flagging, not a discrepancy in the claim itself: the Winterfell README's Lamport+ table disagrees with the paper's Table 3 at the same n and same 123-bit level (n=1024: 20.5 sec / 7.6 GB / 152 KB in the README vs 25.7 sec / 9.5 GB / 165 KB in the paper). Two different published measurements of nominally the same thing; never blend them.

CONSEQUENCE FOR POSITIONING — the round-1 conclusion SURVIVES and gets stronger. The scheme is Lamport+, a w=2 Winternitz OTS instantiated with Rescue-Prime, explicitly chosen because of "its efficient encoding in ZKP systems". It is not a FIPS scheme, not NIST-standardized, and not even SHA-2/SHAKE-based — it is an arithmetization-friendly hash chosen to make the AIR cheap, which is the exact opposite of the constraint a FIPS scheme imposes (ML-DSA/SLH-DSA are locked to SHAKE256/SHA-2, which is hostile to a prime-field AIR). The paper's own Table 4 mentions Falcon-512 and Dilithium3 ONLY as concatenated-size comparisons, with no in-circuit verification. So: PQ-signature-in-STARK prior art is real and must be cited; FIPS-scheme-in-general-purpose-zkVM remains the empty cell. And the flat-amortization result is prior art — the linear-in-N prover time/RAM and logarithmic proof size are stated explicitly in the Winterfell README, and are visible in the paper's Table 3.

WHAT I DID GET. (1) Bibliographic + abstract, from the Springer landing page, verbatim: "The adoption of NIST's post-quantum cryptographic standards has accelerated the shift toward quantum-resistant systems. While many protocols like TLS, IKEv2, and Signal can integrate these standards, others still rely on primitives lacking efficient post-quantum replacements. One such primitive is the BLS signature scheme, widely used in proof-of-stake (PoS) blockchains for its ability to aggregate multiple signatures into a single compact one, reducing communication and verification overhead. Recent research has proposed post-quantum aggregate signatures to replace BLS, but existing schemes remain impractical due to high memory usage and slow aggregation. While prior solutions produce smaller signatures (hundreds of KB), they require hundreds of gigabytes of RAM to aggregate 2^10 signatures. In contrast, our scheme generates slightly larger signatures (2-3 MB) but drastically reduces aggregation time and can handle up to 2^16 signatures on a standard laptop, making it significantly more practical." Title: "HAPPIER: Hash-Based, Aggregatable, Practical Post-quantum Signatures Implemented Efficiently with Risc0". Authors: Arda Saygan, Muhammed Said Gündoğan, Atakan Arslan, Mehmet Emin Gönen. Venue: LightSec 2025 (6th Intl. Workshop on Lightweight Cryptography for Security and Privacy, Istanbul, Türkiye, September 1-2, 2025), LNCS vol. 16216. Springer page states "First Online: 31 January 2026". Keywords listed: Aggregate Signatures, Post-quantum Cryptography, Zero-Knowledge Proofs, Recursive SNARK, zkVM, hash-based.

(2) CODE EXISTS AND CONTAINS THE RAW BENCHMARK LOGS. https://github.com/ArdaSaygan/PQ-Aggregation-via-Recursive-SNARKs, described verbatim as "Project repository for the paper \"HAPPIER: ...\"", Rust, created 2025-04-15, last commit be73fdbf1c8e881baeccbeeb4d1320846c1e6d4b dated 2025-04-18 08:54:06 +0300, message "fix: removed tubitak". README carries "> ⚠️ **Warning:** This is a research prototype. It has **not** been audited...". Requires risc0-r0vm version 1.2.5.

ANSWER TO THE ONE QUESTION. PROVER TIME vs BATCH SIZE: YES, effectively tabulated (as a matrix of log files). run_test.sh sweeps `for n in 128 256 512 1024 2048 4096 9192; do for m in 2 4 8`. FinalBenchmark/ holds 19 result files (merge4_sig9192 and merge8_sig9192 are MISSING). Benchmark/XMSS256/ and Benchmark/XMSS256-192/ add N = 8192, 32768, 65376, 65536, 262144. MEMORY vs BATCH SIZE: NO. A grep of the entire repo for "peak memory|max rss|memory usage|[0-9] ?GB|[0-9] ?GiB" across all .rs/.md/.txt returns ZERO hits. No hardware specification appears in any log ("a standard laptop" is never pinned to a CPU/RAM figure anywhere in the artifact).

THE BIG CAVEAT, AND IT IS LOAD-BEARING. The headline number in every log, "Estimated time to aggregate all signatures", is NOT a measurement. src/bin/merge2.rs:188-190 (identically merge4.rs, merge8.rs): `let aggregation_time = (duration_sig_to_proof1 + duration_sig_to_proof2) / 2 + duration_merge_proofs * no_agg_signatures.ilog2() / merging_proofs.ilog2();` i.e. mean(one leaf proof) + (one merge node) * log2(N)/log2(arity). It counts ONE leaf proof and ONE merge per tree level, so it is an idealised infinitely-parallel critical path, not wall clock. Worse, every single run only ever aggregates TWO DISTINCT SIGNATURES: merge4.rs:145-148 sets `acc_1 = acc00.clone(); acc_2 = acc01.clone(); acc_3 = acc00.clone(); acc_4 = acc01.clone();` and merge8.rs extends the same clone pattern to acc_8, and correspondingly EVERY log line reads `Bitfield: 0b00000011` (2 bits set) regardless of N. NUM_VOTERS is a build-time constant (data-types/build.rs -> num_voters.rs) that only widens the bitfield / public-key list inside the guest, which is why the measured leaf time is nearly flat in N (490-620 s across N=128..9192 in FinalBenchmark).

SCHEME IDENTITY, corrected vs the brief. It is NOT RFC 8391 XMSS. data-types/src/lib.rs imports `hashsig::signature::generalized_xmss::GeneralizedXMSSSignatureScheme` with the comment "Copied from https://github.com/b-wagn/hash-sig/blob/main/src/signature/generalized_xmss/instantiations_sha.rs", i.e. the b-wagn generalized-XMSS used by the post-quantum-Ethereum hash-based multisig line of work. discrepancies:
Several, and they matter.

1. THE BRIEF SAID "XMSS". Wrong in the strict sense. The code uses b-wagn/hash-sig `GeneralizedXMSSSignatureScheme` with Winternitz encoding, SHA tweak hash, HASH_LEN 24 - i.e. generalized XMSS from the post-quantum-Ethereum hash-based-multisig line, not RFC 8391 XMSS. Anyone citing this as "XMSS" without qualification will be corrected.

2. THE BRIEF SAID "up to 2^16 signatures ... on a laptop" READS AS A MEASURED END-TO-END AGGREGATION. It is not. Every published run aggregates exactly TWO distinct signatures (`Bitfield: 0b00000011` in 100% of logs; merge4/merge8 pad with `acc00.clone()`/`acc01.clone()`). The batch-size axis is realised only as a build-time constant that widens the in-guest bitfield and public-key list. The per-N totals are produced by the closed-form `mean(leaf) + merge * log2(N)/log2(arity)`, an idealised infinitely-parallel critical path. This is a materially weaker claim than "we aggregated 65536 signatures".

3. ABSTRACT SAYS "2-3 MB" SIGNATURES; THE ARTIFACT MOSTLY DOES NOT SHOW THAT. Only the XMSS256-192 merge2 configuration lands in range (2404188 bytes at N=8192, 3230448 bytes at N=32768). The FinalBenchmark sweep the README points at reports 7148152-7987660 bytes for merge2, 13457820-15655740 for merge4, and 27637964-30299144 for merge8, and 30273452 bytes for merge4 at N=262144 - up to ~10x the abstract's headline. Either the paper reports only the best configuration, or applies a compression step not present in these logs. Cannot adjudicate without the paper.

4. THE ABSTRACT ATTACKS PRIOR WORK ON MEMORY ("hundreds of gigabytes of RAM") YET PUBLISHES NO MEMORY FIGURE OF ITS OWN anywhere in the artifact. If the paper's tables do contain RAM numbers, they were not derived from any instrumentation visible in the released code.

5. TWO CELLS OF THEIR OWN SWEEP ARE MISSING: run_test.sh requests merge4_sig9192 and merge8_sig9192; neither file exists in FinalBenchmark/. Also note "9192" is itself an apparent typo for 8192 in their own script.

6. "up to 2^16" vs LOG_LIFETIME=16: the XMSS key lifetime is also 2^16, and one benchmark uses N=262144 (2^18) which exceeds it. The abstract's 2^16 is ambiguous between batch size and key lifetime.
followups:
- NOVELTY IMPACT, stated plainly: HAPPIER does NOT fully occupy the cell you were worried about, but it dents it. It publishes a prover-time-vs-batch-size sweep for a hash-based PQ signature aggregated inside RISC Zero across N = 128 to 262144 and merge arity 2/4/8. It does NOT publish memory vs batch size, does NOT publish hardware, and does NOT actually prove more than 2 signatures per run. The defensible PQ-STARK-BENCH differentiators against it remain: (a) measured wall clock for real batches rather than a log2 extrapolation, (b) peak RSS, (c) named hardware, (d) more than one prover (they are RISC Zero only), (e) FIPS schemes (ML-DSA-44 / Falcon / SLH-DSA) rather than generalized XMSS, (f) the classical-vs-PQ precompile-asymmetry disclosure. Do NOT claim "first prover-cost-vs-batch-size for a PQ signature in a zkVM" - between ePrint 2021/1048 and this, that claim is dead.
- The pinned toolchain is risc0-r0vm 1.2.5 (April 2025). Reproducing HAPPIER on RISC Zero v3.0.6 is itself a publishable delta, and the repo is small enough (one `cargo run --release --bin merge<N>`, NUM_VOTERS env var) to actually re-run and instrument for RSS. That would let PQ-STARK-BENCH publish the memory row they omitted, on named hardware, and check the 2-3 MB claim.
- Their guest uses tiny_keccak Sha3 and the RISC Zero Sha2 accelerator ("4726 Sha2 calls, 349724 cycles, (6.06%)"), consistent with the round-1 finding that RISC Zero has no SHAKE precompile.
- Institutional affiliation is TÜBİTAK BİLGEM (inferred from the last commit message "fix: removed tubitak"); a Turkish-language or institutional repository copy of the full text may exist and is the remaining untried avenue.
- Adjacent, surfaced en route and worth checking separately: eprint.iacr.org/2025/055 "Hash-Based Multi-Signatures for Post-Quantum Ethereum" (also at cic.iacr.org/p/2/1/13) - this is almost certainly the b-wagn/hash-sig scheme HAPPIER builds on, and is the likely source of the "hundreds of gigabytes" comparison target.

what_the_source_actually_says:
ALL THREE URLs ARE LIVE (fetched 2026-07-23). Repo exists and is MIT.

=== 1. SCHEME: NOT a FIPS scheme. This is the decisive finding. ===
https://github.com/leanEthereum/leanBench README line 3, verbatim: "**Performance benchmarks for leanSig and leanVM across hardware.**" No ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, FN-DSA, Falcon or Dilithium string appears anywhere in the README (grepped).
leanSig README (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/leanEthereum/leanSig/main/README.md) describes itself verbatim as "a *prototypical* Rust implementation of (synchronized) signatures based on tweakable hash functions and incomparable encodings", implementing "a generic framework from [this paper](https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/055.pdf), which builds XMSS-like hash-based signatures from a primitive called incomparable encodings", with Poseidon-based hardcoded instantiations, and states the code "has *not been audited and is not meant to be used in production*".
The result JSON confirms the primitive at the data level: per-workload meta carries "raw_xmss": 125 and per-node stats carry "n_xmss": 125 and "poseidons": 21081. So: purpose-built generalized-XMSS-over-Poseidon (leanSig), a synchronized/stateful hash-based scheme designed for Ethereum's own consensus signature. It is NOT ML-DSA/SLH-DSA/FN-DSA and it is not intended to be.

=== 2. PROVERS / HARDWARE ===
ONE prover only: leanVM (README: "**leanVM aggregation** at `LOG_INV_RATE_PROD=2`"; formerly leanMultisig; JSON key "leanmultisig_sha" kept for back-compat, branch "devnet5"). Two API modes via `--features`: "api-leansig (devnet4 / devnet5 leanVM, default) or api-xmss (leanVM main)" — both are leanVM, not different zkVMs. No SP1, RISC Zero, OpenVM, Jolt code path exists in the harness.
Hardware: 101 committed result files under results/, spanning 2026-04-28T07-23-46Z to 2026-06-18T17-09-44Z, across exactly 7 machine fingerprints: 7acfb79a54 (20 runs), 6dd7bdf4d4 (19), 934230ae43 (19), cb2db40233 (19), a0805c1f4c (12), 187dd62dc8 (9), 10a2338d23 (3). Machine block is verbatim e.g. {"fingerprint":"934230ae43","cpu_model":"INTEL(R) XEON(R) PLATINUM 8581C CPU @ 2.30GHz","cpu_arch":"x86_64","physical_cores":2,"logical_cores":4,"memory_gb":14.6,"os":"Linux 6.17.0-1018-gcp","label":"c4-standard-4"}. GCP-only cloud runner ("GCP is the only provider currently supported — contributions to add others are welcome"), matrix c4-8 / c4-16 / c4-32 after commit 6ea8e00 dropped c4-standard-4.

=== 3. METRICS + ACTUAL NUMBERS ===
Published per workload: raw samples_ns array, timing{n, mean_ns, stddev_ns, min_ns, p5_ns, p50_ns, p95_ns, max_ns}, resources{cpu_percent{mean,peak}, rss_bytes{mean,peak}, n_samples, interval_ms}, meta{log_inv_rate, proof_kib_by_path, proof_kib_leaf, proof_kib_root, raw_xmss, topology, reports[].nodes[].stats{cycles, dots, memory, n_xmss, poseidons, proof_kib, samples, time_ci_secs, time_secs}}. NO dollar cost metric is published.
LOUD CAVEAT: https://bench.leanroadmap.org/ served NO numbers to WebFetch — it is a client-side-JS Chart.js site, so the fetched HTML contains only framing text ("One chart per headline workload — y-axis is wall-clock mean on the chosen machine, points are individual commits plotted at first-bench timestamp"). I could not read a single figure off the dashboard. === 4. JSON SCHEMA (top level) ===
{"run_id": "2026-06-18T17-09-44Z__934230ae43", "timestamp": "2026-06-18T17:09:44+00:00", "machine": {fingerprint, cpu_model, cpu_arch, physical_cores, logical_cores, memory_gb, os, kernel, label}, "toolchain": {"rustc": "...", "git_shas": {"leansig_sha","leanmultisig_sha"}, "branches": {"leansig_branch","leanmultisig_branch"}}, "workloads": [ {name, unit:"ns", samples_ns[], timing{}, resources{}, meta{}} ], "notes": "", "leanvm_commit_ts": "..."}. Determinism: "Runner uses a deterministic seed (`--seed`, default `0xC0FFEE`) for all RNG draws — keygen entropy, message generation, and per-iteration key variation." Seed-pinning claim CONFIRMED verbatim.

=== 5. CONTRIBUTIONS ===
Explicitly open, verbatim: "## Contribute to the public dataset / The site is the union of every committed run file. If your machine isn't already represented — different CPU generation, ARM, unusual core count, atypical OS — your numbers fill a gap." Process: run `uv run bench` or `uv run remote-bench`, "The result lands in `results/<timestamp>__<fingerprint>.json`. Don't edit it.", "Commit and open a PR against `main`."
BUT the invitation is scoped to NEW HARDWARE for THEIR workload, not new schemes or new provers. There is no scheme or prover dimension in the schema at all (toolchain.git_shas only has leansig_sha + leanmultisig_sha). Our ML-DSA-in-SP1/RISC-Zero numbers have no valid slot in that file format and would be rejected as out-of-scope. The one adjacent open door is "GCP is the only provider currently supported — contributions to add others are welcome" (cloud provider, not prover).

MIT. LICENSE file first lines verbatim: "MIT License" / "Copyright (c) 2026 lean Ethereum".

"Live" is generous. Last commit 26f79cc0 dated 2026-06-20T11:03:51Z; newest result file 2026-06-18. That is ~5 weeks stale as of 2026-07-23. === 8. ROADMAP TRACK ===
https://leanroadmap.org/ does list an in-progress track "Post-Quantum Signature Aggregation with zkVMs", Progress "50%", described as exploring "minimal zero-knowledge virtual machines (zkVMs) specifically optimized for signature aggregation, including various options like Binus M3, SP1, KRU, STU, Jolt, and OpenVM" (six names; "Binus M3" is how the fetched page text read, near-certainly Binius M3 — I did not see it rendered). Milestones fetched: "Benchmark hash in SNARK (Plonky3, STwo, Binius, Hashcaster)" Feb 2025; "Hashcaster exploration work" Feb 2025; "Snarkify hash-based signature aggregation with SP1 & OpenVM" Feb 2025; plus unmarked "Explore GKR style provers", "Explore WHIR", "More explorations over binary field techniques". The site references leanBench as "Cross-hardware performance tracker" at bench.leanroadmap.org.
discrepancies:
Four material corrections to the brief.

(1) "post-quantum hash-based signature" is true but dangerously vague — it is leanSig / generalized XMSS over Poseidon, a purpose-built research prototype for Ethereum consensus, explicitly "not been audited and is not meant to be used in production". It is NOT ML-DSA, SLH-DSA or FN-DSA. leanBench therefore does NOT occupy our cell; it is adjacent, not overlapping. Our FIPS-scheme cell stays empty.

(2) "six proving machines" is a CONFLATION of two different artifacts. leanBench itself measures exactly ONE prover, leanVM (two API feature-flags of the same VM). The six names (Binus/Binius M3, SP1, KRU, STU, Jolt, OpenVM) live on leanroadmap.org as an aspirational 50%-progress track, and its zkVM milestones ("Snarkify hash-based signature aggregation with SP1 & OpenVM") are dated Feb 2025 with no linked numbers. Do not describe leanBench as a multi-prover comparison — it is single-prover, multi-hardware.

(3) "live" overstates it. Last commit 2026-06-20T11:03:51Z, newest result 2026-06-18, ~5 weeks cold on the 2026-07-23 access date; 1 GitHub star, effectively one active contributor.

(4) The dashboard at https://bench.leanroadmap.org/ publishes NO numbers in fetchable HTML — it is client-side Chart.js. Anyone citing "the dashboard says X" without opening a browser is citing something they cannot have read. All figures I quote come from the committed results/*.json, not the rendered page. Also note the dashboard/README publish no dollar-cost metric, so it is not a cost benchmark.

Accurate in the brief: seed-pinned (0xC0FFEE, verbatim), flat batch sizes 125/250/500/1000, tree fan-in 2/4/8 over 125/250/500-sig leaves, 13 aggregate variants, results-as-JSON-per-hardware-fingerprint, open to outside contributions, MIT.

followups:
COLLABORATION ASSESSMENT. Distribution channel: weak right now (1 star, ~5 weeks cold, one active committer). Methodological sibling: strong, and worth copying rather than competing with.

Things to steal from their schema, cheaply and deliberately:
- coarse machine fingerprint as a stable grouping key + filename convention `<ISO8601>__<fingerprint>.json`. Their stated rationale, verbatim: "The fingerprint is coarse by design — OS release and kernel changes don't break machine grouping."
- keep raw samples_ns alongside the derived timing block (n/mean/stddev/min/p5/p50/p95/max). Cheap, and it makes our numbers re-analysable.
- resources{cpu_percent{mean,peak}, rss_bytes{mean,peak}, n_samples, interval_ms} — and copy their disclosure that "CPU percentage is summed across logical cores — a fully-utilized 16-core machine reports 1600%, not 100%".
- their honesty section is a template we should match: "No turbo / governor pinning baked in", and the warmup caveat that the aggregation timer "returns total wall-clock including upstream one-time setup (DFT twiddles, bytecode init) on the first call".
Where we MUST diverge: their file has no scheme axis and no prover axis (toolchain.git_shas is hardcoded to leansig_sha + leanmultisig_sha). We need scheme{name, param_set, spec_ref} and prover{name, version, backend: cpu|cuda|metal, precompiles_used[]} as first-class keys. Adding those as a superset keeps us readable by anyone used to their format without pretending compatibility we don't have.

Positioning consequence (important): leanBench is the strongest available third-party evidence for our flat-amortization claim, and it is NOT a competitor for the FIPS cell. Cite it as prior art in the same breath as ePrint 2021/1048 — "Ethereum's own 2026 harness measures the same shape for a hash-based scheme; nobody has done it for ML-DSA in a general-purpose zkVM." Also note their leaf/root proof_kib columns give us a ready-made vocabulary for recursion topology that we should not reinvent.

Open threads not chased: the leanroadmap milestone "Snarkify hash-based signature aggregation with SP1 & OpenVM" (Feb 2025) may point at real SP1/OpenVM numbers somewhere — worth a dedicated round. Also unverified: whether bench.leanroadmap.org's rendered charts show anything beyond what is in results/*.json (needs a real browser; WebFetch cannot execute its JS). And leanSig itself is active (updated 2026-07-14) while leanBench is not, so the pinned SHAs on the dashboard are likely already behind upstream.

what_the_source_actually_says:
REPO IS REAL AND ACTIVE. OpenZeppelin/cairo-pq-verifiers, id 1279127346, description "Post Quantum verifiers candidates for a custom Account implementation on Starknet", language Cairo, 4 stars, 2 forks, 3 open issues, not archived, default branch main, MIT license. Verified via GitHub API (search_repositories, get_file_contents, list_commits) and raw.githubusercontent.com, not via a page summarizer.

1. THE FIVE FALCON VARIANTS AND THE EXACT STEP COUNTS EXIST. README.md "Snapshot" table, verbatim rows (columns: Crate | Measurement | L2 gas | Steps | % of gas cap):

| `ecdsa_stark` | verify (classical control) | 30,855 | 152 | 0.03% |
| `falcon_512` | verify, hint variant (BLAKE2s) | 12,809,640 | 104,854 | 12.8% |
| `falcon_512` | verify, direct variant (BLAKE2s) | 23,005,760 | 205,781 | 23.0% |
| `falcon_512` | verify, Poseidon variant (native) | 12,045,449 | 97,681 | 12.0% |
| `falcon_512` | verify, SHAKE-256 variant (standard) | 50,163,668 | 312,892 | 50.2% |
| `falcon_512` | verify, SHAKE-256 direct variant (standard) | 60,359,788 | 413,819 | 60.4% |
| `bench_targets` | ECDSA-STARK account, inside `__validate__` | 160,795 | 1,437 | 0.16% |
| `bench_targets` | Falcon-512 hint account, inside `__validate__` | 14,380,100 | 119,124 | 14.4% |
| `bench_targets` | Falcon-512 direct account, inside `__validate__` | 24,525,310 | 219,565 | 24.5% |
| `bench_targets` | Falcon-512 Poseidon account, inside `__validate__` | 13,615,909 | 111,950 | 13.6% |
| `bench_targets` | Falcon-512 SHAKE-256 account, inside `__validate__` | 51,733,728 | 327,157 | 51.7% |
| `bench_targets` | Falcon-512 SHAKE-256 direct account, inside `__validate__` | 61,878,938 | 427,598 | 61.9% |
| `ntt` | forward 512-point transform | 8,895,740 | 81,826 | 8.9% |
| `ntt` | forward transform, unreduced output | 7,832,270 | 73,587 | 7.8% |
| `ntt` | generated Falcon-512 transform, felt input | 2,137,010 | 15,635 | 2.1% |
| `ntt` | generated Falcon-512 transform, `u16` input | 2,137,010 | 15,635 | 2.1% |
| `ntt` | forward + inverse roundtrip | 22,725,980 | 211,044 | 22.7% |
| `ml_dsa_44` | verify | stub (not yet measured) | — | — |
| `poseidon_wots` | verify | stub (not yet measured) | — | — |

The asserted range 97,681 (min, Poseidon) to 413,819 (max, SHAKE-256 direct) is exactly right for the five bare-verify Falcon rows. ECDSA control = 152 steps / 30,855 L2 gas.

2. CONFORMANCE — THE STRATEGIC POINT IS CONFIRMED, WITH ONE IMPORTANT NUANCE. The repo is explicit and self-labels. README: "The variants otherwise differ only in the on-chain hash-to-point that binds the message: - **BLAKE2s** (hint and direct): a non-standard XOF swap via the `core::blake` builtin. Nearly the cheapest, but needs a matching signer. - **Poseidon**: a native `hades_permutation` squeeze. Message-binding, the cheapest variant (marginally below BLAKE2s), and the most STARK-proving-friendly since it uses native field arithmetic. Also non-standard. - **SHAKE-256**: the standard Falcon hash-to-point, interoperable with any compliant signer (e.g. falcon.py)."

So: 3 of 5 variants (BLAKE2s hint, BLAKE2s direct, Poseidon) are NOT standards-conformant; only the 2 SHAKE-256 variants are. schemes.json `standardized` fields verbatim: falcon_512 and falcon_512_direct = "draft (FIPS 206); hash-to-point XOF swapped to BLAKE2s (non-standard)"; falcon_512_poseidon = "draft (FIPS 206); non-standard Poseidon hash-to-point"; falcon_512_shake and falcon_512_shake_direct = "draft (FIPS 206); standard SHAKE-256 hash-to-point".

The 3.2x gap is stated by the repo itself, not just inferred. README: "Its bare hint verifier uses about 4.2× the gas and 3.2× the steps of the native-Poseidon variant." schemes.json notes give the precise figures: "the bare verifier uses 4.16x the gas and 3.20x the steps of the native-Poseidon variant". 312,892 / 97,681 = 3.2032; 50,163,668 / 12,045,449 = 4.1645. Both check out arithmetically.

Most-conformant variant identified by the repo: falcon_512_shake_direct, "The closest on-chain match to a bare standard Falcon signature: standard SHAKE-256 hash-to-point with the hint-free direct core (s1*h computed on-chain), so only the 31-felt s1||salt is supplied... The FIPS signature carries no hint, so this variant mirrors it most faithfully." That is the 413,819-step / 60,359,788-gas row — 4.24x the steps and 5.01x the gas of the cheapest non-standard variant. NUANCE: the repo is careful that Falcon/FN-DSA is FIPS 206 DRAFT, not final — "Standardization | ... draft, FIPS 206 (standard SHAKE-256; non-standard BLAKE2s / Poseidon)". So "standards-conformant Falcon" here means conformant to the Falcon spec / draft FN-DSA, interoperable with falcon.py, not to a finalized FIPS.

3. ML-DSA-44 IS GENUINELY A STUB — AND WORSE THAN "UNIMPLEMENTED". crates/ml_dsa_44/src/lib.cairo verbatim header: "//! STUB — placeholder for an ML-DSA-44 (CRYSTALS-Dilithium, FIPS 204) verifier. //! NOT a real implementation." The whole body is a length check that returns true for any input of the right shape: `fn verify(message_hash: felt252, public_key: Span<felt252>, signature: Span<felt252>) -> bool { let _ = message_hash; public_key.len() == 43 && signature.len() == 79 }`. crates/ml_dsa_44/README.md: "**Status:** stub (pending implementation)." and "## Current efficiency / No measurements yet: the crate is a stub, so it has no ratchet entries." poseidon_wots is the same pattern ("//! STUB ... NOT a real implementation", returns `public_key.len() == 1 && signature.len() == 560`). Its planned cost note: ML-DSA "cost is expected to be SHAKE-dominated in Cairo: NTT over q=8380417 plus heavy SHAKE-256 hashing" — vendor expectation, NOT measured.

4. LICENSE / MAINTENANCE. MIT ("MIT. See LICENSE."). Actively maintained but young: created 2026-06-24, last commit 2026-07-22 (one day before access), all recent commits by ericnordelo (Eric Nordelo, OpenZeppelin). Recent commit subjects: "Update Disclaimer" (#22, 2026-07-22), "Add Disclaimers" (#21, 2026-07-21), "Further optimizations on NTT" (#6, 2026-07-14). CI enforces a one-way efficiency ratchet: "efficiency_baseline.json pins the cost of every tracked benchmark pair (L2 gas and steps), CI fails any change that raises a number". Prominent self-disclaimer: "**Experimental and unaudited.** This repository is intended for research and benchmarking. The verifiers and account contracts have not undergone an independent security audit."

5. THESE ARE STEPS AND L2 GAS ONLY — NO PROVING TIME ANYWHERE. This is decisive and I verified it three ways. (a) README "What we measure": "For each scheme, the cost of verifying one signature, both alone and inside a real `__validate__`: L2 gas, Cairo steps, signature and key size, contract class size, and where the steps go." (b) README "Method": "Numbers come from Starknet Foundry (gas, steps, builtins), a release build (class size), and cairo-profiler (attribution by function)." (c) I grepped the full 32,777-byte results/results.json for any key matching prov|time|second|ms|sec — ZERO matches.
