discrepancies:
The brief is accurate on every checkable number, but four things need tightening before any of it goes into our writeup.

1. "Five Falcon-512 Cairo variants measured in L2 gas and Cairo steps (asserted range 97,681 to 413,819 steps)" — CORRECT, but the snapshot table has 19 rows, not 6. The five Falcon rows are bare-verify only; there are five MORE Falcon rows for the same variants measured inside `__validate__` (111,950 to 427,598 steps), plus five NTT micro-benchmarks and two stub rows. If we cite "413,819" as the ceiling we should say "bare verify", because the in-`__validate__` ceiling is 427,598.

2. "a SHAKE-256-conformant variant costs 312,892 steps vs 97,681 for a Poseidon-native variant, a ~3.2x gap" — CORRECT and, better than asserted, the gap is stated BY the repo itself ("3.20x the steps", "4.16x the gas"), so we are not doing an inference OpenZeppelin would dispute. But 312,892 is the SHAKE *hint* variant. The variant OpenZeppelin itself calls the closest match to a bare FIPS signature is falcon_512_shake_direct at 413,819 steps — 4.24x Poseidon, not 3.2x. Quoting 3.2x actually UNDERSTATES the conformance penalty for the most faithful variant.

3. "The ML-DSA verifier is asserted to be STUBBED (not implemented)" — CORRECT, and stronger than stated. It is not merely unimplemented; the stub `verify` ignores `message_hash` entirely and returns true for any 43-felt key + 79-felt signature. Same for poseidon_wots. So the Round 1 finding that ML-DSA-44 is the empty cell survives contact with the most credible-looking counterexample: OpenZeppelin, a top-tier auditor, has an ML-DSA-44 crate with a NIST reference and a planned encoding and STILL has zero measurements for it.

4. STEPS ARE NOT PROVING SECONDS — and the brief was right to flag it. This repo publishes NO proving time, NO prover, NO hardware, NO peak RAM, NO proof size. Its own "Method" is Starknet Foundry + cairo-profiler, and its own README disclaims that even its gas numbers are not fees (real fees run "about 1.7× and 2.7× these values"). It therefore does NOT compete with PQ-STARK-BENCH's headline metric; it is a Cairo-VM execution-cost study of on-chain verification inside `__validate__`, a different axis entirely. Any chart of ours that puts 97,681 next to a prover-second number would be a category error.

PROCESS WARNING, worth flagging loudly. My first pass used WebFetch on the GitHub HTML page and its summarizer returned a CONFIDENT, WELL-FORMATTED 4-ROW TABLE that SILENTLY DROPPED both `direct` variants — including the 413,819-step row, i.e. the single most strategically important number here. It also asserted "Falcon-512 ... five variants with different hash-to-point functions", which is wrong: there are three hash-to-point functions across five variants (the hint/direct axis is the NTT core, not the hash). Had I stopped there I would have reported the claim as PARTIALLY-CONFIRMED and missed the strongest evidence for our own positioning. Everything above is instead from the GitHub API and raw.githubusercontent.com. Recommend we treat WebFetch summaries of tabular repo content as unusable for benchmark extraction and always pull raw files.

STRATEGIC READ: the conformance axis is REAL and DEFENSIBLE, with one caveat we must state ourselves. The fast published Falcon numbers do relax the standard — 3 of the 5 variants (both BLAKE2s, and the cheapest-of-all Poseidon at 97,681) swap the hash-to-point XOF and are self-labelled "non-standard" and needing "a matching signer". BUT: OpenZeppelin does NOT hide this, does not lead with the fast number, ships two SHAKE-256-conformant variants, and quantifies the penalty itself. So our contribution cannot be "we caught them relaxing the standard" — that framing would be unfair and easy to refute. The honest and stronger framing is that conformance-vs-cost is quantified on-chain by OpenZeppelin but NOT quantified in prover terms by anyone. Also note FIPS 206 is DRAFT, so "standards-conformant Falcon" means Falcon-spec / falcon.py-interoperable, not final-FIPS; we should never write "FIPS-conformant Falcon" unqualified.

published_date: 2026-06-30 (StarkWare roadmap post, "Published on: June 30, 2026"); supporting: EF blog "lean Ethereum" 2025-07-31; ethereum.org quantum-resistance page "Page last update: April 9, 2026"; accessed 2026-07-23
what_the_source_actually_says:
CONFIRMED, and stronger/more recent than the brief implies. Fetched 2026-07-23.

(1) OFFICIAL STARKNET PQ ROADMAP — EXISTS. StarkWare blog, "The Architecture Advantage: Starknet's Quantum Readiness Roadmap", "Published on: June 30, 2026". Verbatim: "Today, StarkWare presents its PQ roadmap for Starknet from a position of architectural strength." / "The roadmap is organized into three phases. The first two are fully within Starknet's control and carry committed time estimates. The third documents dependencies on Ethereum's own migration timeline."
- Phase 1 "Securing All New Activity": "substituting Pedersen hashing, which inherits elliptic-curve assumptions, for BLAKE2, which does not", across "the state, chain environment, and consensus layers". Surfaces: state commitment layer, contract address derivation, OS config hash. Verbatim estimate: "OS config hash live in early July; other items - approximately two months from start." Also: "all new features will require quantum-safe implementations. These items include, among others, implementing consensus signatures onchain using PQ signature schemes, such as Falcon-512."
- Phase 2 "Bringing Legacy Contracts Forward": contract-storage-level PQ, migration toolkit "actively researching". Verbatim: "approximately one month after Phase 1 for easy path on StarkWare side; TBD on dapps side." And: "No action is required of users and developers who wish to continue operating as is; only teams that wish to be PQ-secure will need to use the migration toolkit."
- Phase 3 "External Dependencies": "Bridge secp Syscalls. The Cairo VM's secp256k1/r1 syscalls, which support L1-to-L2 messaging and the Ethereum bridge, are quantum-vulnerable." Estimate: "1 month post-Ethereum migration". Plus "Blob data availability ... anchored by a KZG commitment, which is quantum-vulnerable. A KZG compromise would affect data availability transparency, but not state-transition soundness, which remains protected by the STARK proof. This surface is Ethereum's to migrate."
- Explicit non-commitment disclaimer, verbatim: "All protocol-level changes to the Starknet network are subject to approval through Starknet's governance process. No timeline or outcome described herein is guaranteed."

(2) WHAT STARKNET ACCOUNTS USE TODAY. The protocol mandates NO signature scheme. Starknet docs (https://docs.starknet.io/learn/protocol/cryptography, accessed 2026-07-23) define the STARK curve over the STARK field F_P with P = 2^251 + 17*2^192 + 1, gives alpha = 1, beta = 3141592653589793238462643383279502884197169399375105820974944592307816406665, curve order 3618502788666131213697322783095070105526743751716087489154079457884512865583, and states verbatim: "the generator point G in the Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA) implementation that is used in Cairo" and "The STARK curve is commonly used in smart contracts, but is not distinguished by the Starknet protocol."
Starknet accounts doc (https://docs.starknet.io/learn/protocol/accounts.md): accounts are contracts implementing __validate__/__execute__; "Determine what it means for a signature to be valid ... also known as signature abstraction". No default scheme named.
De-facto default, OpenZeppelin Contracts for Cairo 3.x accounts doc (https://docs.openzeppelin.com/contracts-cairo/3.x/accounts, accessed 2026-07-23), verbatim: "usually most account implementations validate transactions using the Stark curve which is the most efficient way" and "Besides the Stark-curve account, OpenZeppelin Contracts for Cairo also offers Ethereum-flavored accounts that use the secp256k1 curve for signature validation."
=> ANSWER: today the overwhelmingly common Starknet account signature is ECDSA on the STARK curve — a 251-bit prime-field elliptic curve, i.e. STARK-FRIENDLY but NOT post-quantum (Shor breaks it). Alternatives in production use are secp256k1/secp256r1, also not PQ.

(3) PQ ACCOUNTS EXIST ON STARKNET TODAY (opt-in). StarkWare roadmap verbatim: "Starknet users can deploy a PQ wallet today, which S2morrow has already demonstrated, with their own Falcon-512 account implementation in Cairo. Additionally, OpenZeppelin has begun working on a PQ account contract for Starknet, which is expected to be released soon." s2morrow.xyz (accessed 2026-07-23) is a live demo site: "Live on Starknet / Quantum-proof wallets. No hard fork required."

(4) ETHEREUM'S STATED PQ DIRECTION. EF blog "lean Ethereum", Justin Drake, July 31, 2025 (https://blog.ethereum.org/2025/07/31/lean-ethereum), verbatim: "Hash-based cryptography is emerging as the ideal foundation for lean Ethereum" ... "CL: hash-based aggregate signatures upgrade BLS signatures / DL: hash-based DAS commitments upgrade KZG commitments / EL: hash-based real-time zkVMs upgrade EVM re-execution". Note it is explicitly labelled "a Drake take" and "A technical deep dive into hash-based post-quantum signatures and SNARKs will follow."
ethereum.org/roadmap/future-proofing/quantum-resistance/ ("Page last update: April 9, 2026"), verbatim: "The Ethereum Foundation has a dedicated post-quantum research team, and a structured 'Lean Ethereum' roadmap targeting 2029 for full post-quantum protection". Four vulnerable areas named: consensus BLS, KZG data availability, ECDSA account signatures, application-layer ZK proofs.
- Account signatures, verbatim: "Every standard Ethereum account (externally owned account, or ) uses ECDSA on the secp256k1 curve to sign transactions." Approach: "Rather than a single protocol-wide migration, Ethereum plans to use account abstraction (specifically EIP-8141, being considered for Hegota in second half of 2026) to give users signature agility."
- Consensus: "Ethereum will replace BLS signatures with leanXMSS, a hash-based signature scheme for validators" plus "leanVM: A minimal zkVM ... for SNARK-based signature aggregation."
- Proof systems, verbatim: "STARKs, which rely on hash functions rather than elliptic curves, are already quantum-resistant and are used by several rollups."
- Threat timing, verbatim: "In March 2026, Google Quantum AI published research estimating that breaking 256-bit elliptic curve cryptography (the type Ethereum uses for account signatures) could require roughly 1,200 logical qubits." and "the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) anticipates deprecating ECDSA by 2030 and disallowing it by 2035."

(5) IS THE MOTIVATING GAP REAL? Yes, but it must be stated with three qualifications or it is attackable:
(a) REAL, and citable: STARK proofs are hash-based (StarkWare: "STARKs ... rely on collision-resistant hash functions, not elliptic-curve arithmetic"; ethereum.org: "STARKs ... are already quantum-resistant"), while the account/authentication layer is elliptic-curve for essentially all deployed accounts on both chains. ethereum.org itself frames the vulnerability as concentrated at accounts; StarkWare verbatim: "The quantum vulnerability facing most major blockchains is concentrated at the authentication layer".
(b) OVERSIMPLIFIED in the direction of flattering the settlement layer. StarkWare's own roadmap concedes non-PQ pieces INSIDE settlement: Pedersen hashing "inherits elliptic-curve assumptions" and was still in the state commitment / address derivation / OS config hash until Phase 1 (BLAKE2 swap, in flight as of July 2026); the L1 bridge uses quantum-vulnerable secp256k1/r1 syscalls; and EIP-4844 blob DA is "anchored by a KZG commitment, which is quantum-vulnerable". So "the settlement proof is PQ" is defensible only about the proof system's soundness, NOT about the whole settlement stack.
(c) OVERSIMPLIFIED in the other direction too: on Starknet the account scheme is NOT protocol-fixed ("not distinguished by the Starknet protocol"), and PQ accounts (Falcon-512) are deployable today. So the correct phrasing is a DEFAULTS/DEPLOYED-BASE gap and a PROVER-COST gap, not a protocol-impossibility gap. Ethereum is heading the same way via EIP-8141 signature agility.
SAFE FORMULATION: "The proof system securing settlement is already hash-based and post-quantum, while the signatures authorizing the transactions inside it are elliptic-curve on essentially all deployed accounts; both StarkWare and the EF now plan to close that at the account layer via account abstraction / signature agility rather than a protocol-wide swap — which makes the prover cost of verifying PQ signatures inside the proof the binding constraint."

(6) ECOSYSTEM INTEREST RISING — CONFIRMED with dates: EF lean Ethereum 2025-07-31; ethereum.org PQ page updated 2026-04-09 citing a Vitalik roadmap of February 2026 and an EF post-quantum team; StarkWare roadmap 2026-06-30; s2morrow Falcon-512 accounts live.

discrepancies:
The brief is directionally right but three things need correcting before any of it goes into copy:

1. UNDERSTATED RECENCY/STRENGTH. "StarkWare announced a Starknet post-quantum roadmap" reads as a vague 2025-2026 gesture. It is a specific, phased, dated document published 2026-06-30 with per-item time estimates, a named PQ scheme (Falcon-512), a named hash swap (Pedersen -> BLAKE2), and Phase 1 partly already live on mainnet ("OS config hash ... will go live on mainnet in early July"). Cite the date.

2. OVERSTATED AS A COMMITMENT. The post carries an explicit hedge we must quote if we characterize it as committed: "All protocol-level changes to the Starknet network are subject to approval through Starknet's governance process. No timeline or outcome described herein is guaranteed." Phase 3 is not scheduled at all - it is contingent on Ethereum. Calling this a "commitment to be PQ by date X" would be wrong.

3. THE MOTIVATING GAP IS REAL BUT MIS-STATED IF LEFT BARE. "Settlement proof is PQ but account signatures are not" is accurate only about (a) the STARK proof's soundness and (b) the DEPLOYED account base. It is NOT accurate about the whole settlement stack: by StarkWare's own admission Pedersen hashing "inherits elliptic-curve assumptions" and was still in the state commitment/address derivation until Phase 1; the L1 bridge uses quantum-vulnerable secp256k1/r1 syscalls; and blob DA is "anchored by a KZG commitment, which is quantum-vulnerable". It is also NOT accurate as a protocol claim on Starknet: the STARK curve "is not distinguished by the Starknet protocol", and Falcon-512 accounts are deployable today. Ethereum is on the same account-abstraction path (EIP-8141 signature agility). Any chart or headline that says "settlement is PQ, accounts are not" without those qualifiers is refutable by a StarkWare engineer in one sentence.

4. Also note StarkWare's marketing sentence "Starknet's architecture does not rely on quantum-vulnerable cryptography at the account or settlement layers" is, on its face, in tension with the same post's Phase 1 (Pedersen) and Phase 3 (secp syscalls, KZG). Do not quote that sentence as evidence for our gap claim - it argues against having a gap, and it is the weakest sentence in their own document.
